Author: @ZWARBHATTAA
Comment:
Yeti lamo video banaunai pardaina. Simple kura cha. We have two kinds of Nepalese today.

1. Ambitious, needs good lifestyle and quality healthcare. These Nepalese are wise and forward thinking. They have no problem leaving Nepal and settle abroad.

2. Self-contained, very difficultly influenced by outside world and people, happy with an ok life with no effort (or will) to seek for a better one and above all, emotional fools who want to stick to family values and wont let go off their parents in their old days. They stay back. Pollution hos, corruption hos, garibi hos, they will stay back. And it's not that they cant go abroad. They just dont want to.

Choose one. We should respect both kinds of Nepalese.

Author: @binithebillionaire
Comment: I think every Nepalese going abroad now should at least ensure that they carry their culture and national pride with them too. Because it's so easy to be influenced in foreign territory where every culture is different and new. It might be a total shocker and hard to adapt but eventually, it becomes a part of our life. However, if we could also share a bits of our culture, then that would be a great cultural exchange. Our history is so rich that we were never colonized yet most of the foreigners don't even know that Nepal exists. That's fine but what would be even sadder is if our own future generations don't know about our country. It's crucial for us to build a great diaspora for our identity. 🇳🇵

Author: @surajrasaili9323
Comment: The young generation constantly faces significant challenges. Although they have many innovative ideas to contribute to the country, the lack of proper policies makes it difficult to kickstart these ideas. As they grow older, societal expectations to support their families arise, making it challenging to continue pursuing these ideas. Consequently, many are compelled to go abroad for better income, education, or training. The allure of a better world often blinds Nepalese individuals, and the high rates of corruption further discourage them from investing in Nepal.

Few years ago I and my friend decided to stay in Nepal and pursue an undergraduate degree among 2 out of 20 of my friends. I graduated this year and I regret my decision.

I don’t see certainty and sustainability here. If someone hits me on a road, I can’t assure if he’s going to jail. My property, family is all at stake here.

And thus, I am leaving Nepal this year. This totally depends on individual preferences and choices but for me, I don’t see myself truly living my life to the fullest here with all the political chaos.

Author: @samad3251
Comment: While Kuznetsov's theory offers an optimistic framework, its application to Nepal is fraught with challenges. The country's weak and corrupt institutions, fragmented diaspora, and dependency on remittances diminish the likelihood of a transformative role for Nepalese expatriates. Without significant reforms within Nepal to address these structural issues, the potential of the diaspora will remain unrealized, leaving the country to grapple with the long-term consequences of brain drain. I don't see much prospects until Nepali people change their vote.

Author: @rakshitkhadka8493
Comment:
Beautifully well balanced argument on Yevgeny Kuznetsov's theory.

I would like to add one more criticism to it - the replacement that occurs in the absence of the working population.

For example: Indian population replacing and taking over jobs, businesses, properties and positions in the absence of working population in Nepal.

The country does not stand still in vacuum while the diaspora establishes abroad. More likely, it will change into something completely different, leading to more disconnect.
